title: The meeting that should have been a walk
summary: Argue that many serious conversations would go better as a walk than as a sit-down meeting, drawing on specific contrasts you have observed.
topic: When a walk would have been a better meeting
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) arguing that certain conversations work better as a walk than as a meeting room sit-down. Give a concrete example, address a counter-objection, and use the present perfect, modals of past possibility, a relative clause, and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: In four years of project work in Dakar, the conversations I remember as productive almost never happened in the meeting room with the long table and the air conditioning, but in…
If two colleagues need to discuss something genuinely difficult, the worst place to put them is often a room with chairs facing each other, because…
Although there are situations in which a sit-down meeting is the right choice, what I have come to believe is that a surprising number of meetings would have been better as a walk, because…

hintVocab.meaningVi: một cuộc trò chuyện song song bên nhau
duy trì giao tiếp bằng mắt
một cuộc trò chuyện khó khăn
suy nghĩ trong khi di chuyển
nhịp điệu của việc đi bộ
một cuộc trò chuyện hiệu quả

sampleText:
In four years of project work in Dakar, the conversations I remember as genuinely productive almost never happened in the meeting room with the long table and the air conditioning. They happened on the short walk between the office and the bus stop, on the way to lunch, or — most often — in the half-hour a colleague and I sometimes took to walk to a kiosk near the office for coffee. We solved problems on those walks that had refused to move under fluorescent light for three weeks. I used to assume this was just my preference, but I have spoken to enough people in different industries to suspect it is something more general. There are conversations that should never have been sit-down meetings in the first place, and recognising which they are could save organisations enormous amounts of time.

If two colleagues need to discuss something genuinely difficult — disagreement over a decision, a piece of personal feedback, a tension that has been building for a while — the worst place to put them is often a room with chairs facing each other. The face-to-face arrangement, which seems intuitive, in fact maximises the social cost of disagreement. Every pause becomes loaded; every glance has to be managed; the silence is uncomfortable in a way that pushes both people to fill it badly. On a walk, however, the cost of a pause is almost zero. Both people are looking forward, both are moving at the same pace, and the awkwardness of a long silence is absorbed by the simple fact that you are doing something else — putting one foot in front of the other — while the conversation works itself out. A colleague of mine once told me about a long-standing disagreement that had been blocking a project for months. They had tried three meetings. Then they walked together to a different building one morning, and by the time they arrived, the problem must have already begun to dissolve, because both of them came back lighter than they had left.

However, I do not want to overstate this. Some meetings genuinely need a room: those where you must share a screen, take detailed notes, or include people who cannot walk easily. The point is not that walking is universally better, but that it is a surprisingly underused option that we forget to consider. Although the calendar invite always asks 'where is the meeting?', it almost never asks 'does this have to be a meeting at all?'. A culture in which someone could honestly reply, 'Let us walk instead', without it sounding strange, would probably have shorter, calmer, and more useful conversations than one in which every difficult subject is sentenced to the same square room with the same long table. Worth trying, at least once, the next time something is stuck.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal of past deduction: 'must have + past participle'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'By the time they arrived, the problem must have already begun to dissolve' dùng 'must have + quá khứ phân từ' để diễn đạt một suy luận chắc chắn về một việc đã xảy ra trong quá khứ — kết luận hợp lý dựa trên bằng chứng quan sát được sau đó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'By the time they arrived, the problem must have already begun to dissolve' uses 'must have + past participle' to express a strong deduction about a past event — a logical conclusion drawn from later observable evidence.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal of past obligation/regret: 'should never have been + past participle'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'There are conversations that should never have been sit-down meetings in the first place' dùng 'should never have been + danh từ' (cấu trúc rút gọn của 'should never have been + held as') để diễn đạt sự tiếc nuối về một quyết định trong quá khứ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'There are conversations that should never have been sit-down meetings in the first place' uses 'should never have been + noun' (a compressed form of 'should never have been held as') to express regret about a past decision.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trong bốn năm làm việc dự án ở Dakar, những cuộc trò chuyện tôi nhớ là thực sự hiệu quả gần như không bao giờ diễn ra trong phòng họp với chiếc bàn dài và máy lạnh. Chúng diễn ra trên đoạn đi bộ ngắn giữa văn phòng và bến xe buýt, trên đường đi ăn trưa, hoặc — thường nhất — trong nửa giờ mà một đồng nghiệp và tôi đôi khi dành để đi bộ đến một quầy gần văn phòng để uống cà phê. Chúng tôi đã giải quyết những vấn đề trong những lần đi bộ đó đã từ chối nhúc nhích dưới đèn huỳnh quang trong ba tuần. Tôi từng cho rằng đây chỉ là sở thích của tôi, nhưng tôi đã nói chuyện với đủ người trong các ngành khác nhau để nghi ngờ rằng đó là điều gì đó tổng quát hơn. Có những cuộc trò chuyện đáng lẽ chưa bao giờ nên là cuộc họp ngồi xuống ngay từ đầu, và nhận ra những cuộc nào là như vậy có thể tiết kiệm cho các tổ chức một lượng thời gian khổng lồ.

Nếu hai đồng nghiệp cần thảo luận điều gì đó thực sự khó khăn — bất đồng về một quyết định, một phản hồi cá nhân, một căng thẳng đã tích tụ một thời gian — nơi tệ nhất để đặt họ thường là một phòng có ghế đối diện nhau. Cách sắp xếp đối diện, mà có vẻ trực quan, thực ra tối đa hóa chi phí xã hội của sự bất đồng. Mỗi khoảng dừng trở nên nặng nề; mỗi ánh nhìn phải được quản lý; sự im lặng khó chịu theo cách đẩy cả hai người đến chỗ lấp đầy nó một cách tệ. Tuy nhiên, trong một chuyến đi bộ, chi phí của một khoảng dừng gần như bằng không. Cả hai người đều nhìn về phía trước, cả hai đều di chuyển cùng nhịp độ, và sự ngượng ngùng của một khoảng im lặng dài bị hấp thụ bởi sự thật đơn giản là bạn đang làm điều gì khác — đặt chân này trước chân kia — trong khi cuộc trò chuyện tự nó giải quyết. Một đồng nghiệp của tôi từng kể với tôi về một bất đồng kéo dài đã chặn một dự án trong nhiều tháng. Họ đã thử ba cuộc họp. Rồi họ đi bộ cùng nhau đến một tòa nhà khác vào một buổi sáng, và đến khi họ đến, vấn đề hẳn đã bắt đầu tan biến, bởi vì cả hai đều trở về nhẹ nhõm hơn so với khi rời đi.

Tuy nhiên, tôi không muốn nói quá. Một số cuộc họp thực sự cần một căn phòng: những cuộc họp nơi bạn phải chia sẻ màn hình, ghi chép chi tiết, hoặc bao gồm những người không thể đi bộ dễ dàng. Vấn đề không phải là đi bộ là tốt hơn về mọi mặt, mà là nó là một lựa chọn ít được sử dụng đáng ngạc nhiên mà chúng ta quên xem xét. Mặc dù lời mời lịch luôn hỏi 'cuộc họp ở đâu?', nó gần như không bao giờ hỏi 'điều này có cần phải là một cuộc họp không?'. Một văn hóa trong đó ai đó có thể thật lòng trả lời, 'Hãy đi bộ thay thế', mà không nghe lạ, có lẽ sẽ có những cuộc trò chuyện ngắn hơn, bình tĩnh hơn và hữu ích hơn so với một văn hóa nơi mọi chủ đề khó khăn bị tuyên án vào cùng căn phòng vuông với cùng chiếc bàn dài. Đáng thử, ít nhất một lần, lần tới khi điều gì đó bị kẹt.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'must have + past participle' correctly for past deduction?
options: The problem must have already begun to dissolve.
The problem must has already begun to dissolve.
The problem must of already begun to dissolve.
The problem must have already began to dissolve.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Must have + past participle' is the modal of past deduction. The past participle of 'begin' is 'begun', not 'began'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'should never have been + noun' correctly?
options: There are conversations that should never have been sit-down meetings in the first place.
There are conversations that should never have be sit-down meetings in the first place.
There are conversations that should never had been sit-down meetings in the first place.
There are conversations that should never has been sit-down meetings in the first place.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Should + have + been + complement' expresses past regret; 'have' stays as 'have' after the modal.

options: Although the calendar invite always asks 'where is the meeting?', it almost never asks 'does this have to be a meeting at all?'.
Although the calendar invite always asks 'where is the meeting?', but it almost never asks 'does this have to be a meeting at all?'.
Although, the calendar invite always asks 'where is the meeting?' it almost never asks 'does this have to be a meeting at all?'.
The calendar invite always asks 'where is the meeting?' although however it almost never asks 'does this have to be a meeting at all?'.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'however' correctly to introduce a contrasting paragraph?
options: However, I do not want to overstate this.
However I do not want to overstate this.
However; I do not want to overstate this.
However, I do not want to over-stating this.
mcQuestions.explanation: Sentence-initial 'however' takes a comma. After 'want', use 'to + base verb' ('to overstate').

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a non-defining relative clause correctly?
options: The face-to-face arrangement, which seems intuitive, in fact maximises the social cost of disagreement.
The face-to-face arrangement, that seems intuitive, in fact maximises the social cost of disagreement.
The face-to-face arrangement, which seem intuitive, in fact maximises the social cost of disagreement.
The face-to-face arrangement which seems intuitive in fact maximises the social cost of disagreement.
mcQuestions.explanation: Non-defining relative clauses use 'which' (not 'that') and are set off by commas; singular 'arrangement' takes 'seems'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses past perfect correctly inside a comparison?
options: Both of them came back lighter than they had left.
Both of them came back lighter than they have left.
Both of them came back lighter than they had leave.
Both of them came back lighter than they did left.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle' ('left') for an earlier past reference compared to a later past moment.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'used to + base verb' for a past habit correctly?
options: I used to assume this was just my preference.
I use to assume this was just my preference.
I used to assuming this was just my preference.
I was used to assume this was just my preference.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Used to + base verb' for past habit/state. 'Use to' (without -d) is wrong; 'was used to' has a different meaning.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'the worst place to + base verb' correctly?
options: The worst place to put them is often a room with chairs facing each other.
The worst place for put them is often a room with chairs facing each other.
The worst place putting them is often a room with chairs facing each other.
The most worst place to put them is often a room with chairs facing each other.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The + superlative + noun + to + base verb' is the standard structure. 'The most worst' is wrong (double superlative).

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'in the first place'?
options: There are conversations that should never have been sit-down meetings in the first place.
There are conversations that should never have been sit-down meetings in first place.
There are conversations that should never have been sit-down meetings in the first time.
There are conversations that should never have been sit-down meetings at the first place.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In the first place' is a fixed idiom meaning 'originally / from the start'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'a culture in which + clause' correctly?
options: A culture in which someone could honestly reply, 'Let us walk instead', without it sounding strange.
A culture which in someone could honestly reply, 'Let us walk instead', without it sounding strange.
A culture in which someone could honestly reply, 'Let us walk instead', without it to sound strange.
A culture in which someone could honestly replied, 'Let us walk instead', without it sounding strange.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In which + subject + verb' is the standard relative phrase; 'without + it + -ing' is the structure for an unreal subject in a gerund clause.

options: Worth trying, at least once, the next time something is stuck.
Worth try, at least once, the next time something is stuck.
Worth to try, at least once, the next time something is stuck.
Worth of trying, at least once, the next time something is stuck.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Worth + -ing' is the standard structure. 'Worth to' and 'worth of trying' are non-standard.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If walking conversations are often more productive, why do organisations still default to meeting rooms? Is it really about the work, or about the visibility of being 'in a meeting' — and what would have to change for that default to shift?
